steps.title: Pin down the claim that needs support
steps.content: Barboza and Trejos say rural women deliberately sell less frequently because doing so raises their profit per unit sold. The right answer must describe a cost structure in which fewer trips genuinely yield higher per-unit profit.

steps.title: Find the choice that creates that incentive
steps.content: If the cost of bringing goods to market is high but fixed regardless of how much is brought, then waiting to amass more goods spreads that fixed cost over more units, lowering per-unit cost and raising per-unit profit. That directly explains the rural sellers' strategic infrequency.

steps.title: Eliminate the rest
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'fixed percentage of each day's proceeds': a per-day percentage fee is proportional to sales, so consolidating trips would not change per-unit profit. It fails to support the strategic-infrequency claim. ✗
  • Choice B — 'higher prices in cities... more competition': this is about where to sell, not how often, and the competition caveat actually cuts against the profit argument. ✗
  • Choice C — 'smaller initial loans': loan size has no bearing on whether selling less frequently increases per-unit profit. Off-topic. ✗
  • Choice D — 'high but largely independent of the number of goods': a fixed trip cost is exactly the structure that rewards bundling more units per trip, supporting the claim. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
